fields.Identifying_Data_Chief_Complaint: 13-year-old male brought by school psychologist after stating he wishes he 'could just not wake up' following learning disability diagnosis.
fields.Subjective: Patient is a 13-year-old male presenting after expressing to the school psychologist during his IEP meeting that he sometimes wishes he could go to sleep and not wake up. He was just formally diagnosed with dyslexia and dyscalculia after years of struggling academically and feeling 'stupid.' He describes relief at finally having an explanation for his difficulties but also profound sadness about being 'different' and needing special education services. He endorses passive thoughts over the past week of not wanting to be alive, wishing he did not have to face the embarrassment of leaving class for resource support. He explicitly denies any plan, method, or intent to harm himself, clarifying these are more wishes to escape embarrassment than desires to die. Sleep normal, appetite good. He loves skateboarding and has a close group of skater friends who do not attend his school. His mother is very supportive and advocating strongly for appropriate services. He has never had thoughts like this before and has no history of mental health concerns. (School Psychologist Dr. Patricia Moore, PsyD)

Mental_Status_Examination.Thought_Content: Passive suicidal ideation without plan or intent; shame-based

fields.Diagnostic_Impression: Adjustment disorder with depressed mood in reaction to learning disability diagnosis; passive suicidal ideation related to shame.
fields.Psychosocial_Context: Recently diagnosed with dyslexia and dyscalculia; history of academic struggles; strong maternal support; close peer group outside school; engaged in skateboarding community.
Assessment_and_Plan: Low risk: passive SI without plan or intent in context of adjustment to diagnosis and anticipated stigma.
Strong protective factors: supportive mother, peer group, skateboarding interest, good insight.
Normal reaction to diagnosis involving anticipated shame; requires support normalizing learning differences.
Safety plan created with patient and school psychologist.
Referral to counselor specializing in learning differences and adolescent self-esteem.
Connect family with learning disability support groups and advocacy organizations.
School to implement IEP sensitively to minimize peer awareness if patient prefers.
Encourage continued skateboarding and peer connections as protective activity.
Follow-up with outpatient therapist within one week.
Safe for discharge with mother and school psychologist support.
fields.Author: Dr. Patricia Moore, PsyD (School Psychologist)
